threats.title: decodeWellData Copy-Paste Bug: decimal1 Never Defaults to 18, Corrupting All Pool Math
threats.hypothesis_description: In Stable2.decodeWellData, the second default check reads `if (decimal0 == 0) { decimal1 = 18; }` instead of `if (decimal1 == 0) { decimal1 = 18; }`. After the first block sets decimal0 to 18, the second condition is always false. When data encodes decimal1 as 0, it stays 0, causing getScaledReserves to scale reserves[1] by 10^18 instead of 10^0. This corrupts LP supply, reserve, rate, and all ratio calculations. Any Well deployed with decimal1=0 in its data is completely broken.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a deterministic code defect that breaks the mathematical invariant of any pool where the second token's decimal is encoded as 0. Any attacker can exploit the miscalculated reserves to drain the pool through mispriced swaps.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Second if-check tests `decimal0 == 0` instead of `decimal1 == 0`; decimal1 never gets default value of 18

preconditions: A Well is deployed with data encoding where decimal1 = 0 (e.g., abi.encode(6, 0) or abi.encode(0, 0))
The Well uses Stable2 as its well function

initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent for any affected pool from deployment

operational_questions: Are any Wells currently deployed with decimal1=0 in their well data?
Does LibWellUpgradeableConstructor or LibWellConstructor ever encode 0 for decimal values?
What is the exact financial impact of the 10^18 scaling error on swap pricing?
validation_requirements: Call decodeWellData(abi.encode(6, 0)) and verify decimals[1] returns 0 instead of 18
Call calcLpTokenSupply with the bug-affected decimals and compare to expected values
Demonstrate a swap exploit on a pool with this misconfiguration

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify or deploy a Well using Stable2 with data encoding decimal1=0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Calculate the price distortion caused by reserves[1] being scaled by 10^18 extra
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute swaps exploiting the massively distorted invariant to extract value

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All pool operations produce incorrect results; pool is effectively broken
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires redeployment with corrected data or code fix
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All swaps, adds, removes, rate calculations, oracle updates

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic — just execute swaps at distorted prices

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Check wellData of all deployed Stable2 Wells for decimal1=0

threats.title: Missing onlyOwner Guard on upgradeTo/upgradeToAndCall Enables Unauthorized Implementation Swap
threats.hypothesis_description: WellUpgradeable.upgradeTo and upgradeToAndCall override the parent UUPS functions but do not include any msg.sender access control (no onlyOwner modifier). _authorizeUpgrade validates implementation registration in the Aquifer and proxiableUUID, but never checks who is calling. Since Aquifer.boreWell is permissionless, any user can register a malicious implementation and then upgrade any WellUpgradeable proxy to it. Note: this is currently blocked by a separate _authorizeUpgrade logic bug (OPS-H004), but the access control defect is independently critical.
threats.investigation_rationale: The OwnableUpgradeable contract is initialized in init(), providing owner(), but onlyOwner is never applied to the upgrade path. If the _authorizeUpgrade parameter bug (OPS-H004) is ever fixed, this becomes immediately exploitable for full pool takeover.

proof_of_signal.pattern: upgradeTo is `public override` with no onlyOwner modifier; _authorizeUpgrade at lines 52-71 never checks msg.sender

preconditions: _authorizeUpgrade parameter bug (OPS-H004) must be resolved or bypassed
Attacker can bore a well through the same Aquifer with a malicious implementation
Proxy's ERC1967 slot must be set correctly

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: upgradeTo at line 73 has no modifier beyond `override`; _authorizeUpgrade never references msg.sender or owner()
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: OwnableUpgradeable is inherited and initialized but never used for upgrade authorization

operational_questions: Can the _authorizeUpgrade parameter bug be bypassed under any circumstances?
Does the Aquifer have any restrictions on who can bore wells?
Is there any other mechanism blocking unauthorized upgrade calls?
validation_requirements: Confirm non-owner address can call upgradeTo without revert (after fixing OPS-H004)
Verify Aquifer.boreWell is permissionless
Test end-to-end attack: bore malicious impl, upgrade victim proxy, call drain

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy malicious WellUpgradeable with drain() function and valid proxiableUUID
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Bore it through the target Aquifer to register it
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call upgradeTo(maliciousProxy) on victim WellUpgradeable proxy
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Call drain() on the now-upgraded proxy to extract all tokens

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total pool takeover, all funds at risk

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor upgradeTo calls on WellUpgradeable proxies

threats.title: calcReserveAtRatioSwap and calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity Silently Return 0 on Non-Convergence
threats.hypothesis_description: Both calcReserveAtRatioSwap and calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity declare a named return variable `reserve` but never assign it outside the for-loop. If Newton's method fails to converge within 255 iterations, execution falls through the loop and the function returns the Solidity default value of 0. Downstream callers (e.g., Beanstalk oracle, pump integrations) may interpret 0 as a valid reserve, enabling zero-cost swaps or oracle manipulation.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike calcReserve which explicitly reverts on non-convergence, these two critical functions silently return 0. An attacker can craft specific reserve/ratio inputs that prevent convergence, weaponizing the 0 return value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Named return `reserve` is never assigned; for-loop at line 173 has no revert after it; function ends without return statement after loop

preconditions: Attacker can provide reserves/ratios that prevent Newton convergence within 255 iterations
Downstream caller does not validate for 0 return value
Target price is within LUT range (otherwise LUT reverts first)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: calcReserve (line 113) has explicit `revert('did not find convergence')` but calcReserveAtRatioSwap/Liquidity do not

operational_questions: What specific reserve/ratio combinations cause non-convergence?
How does Beanstalk handle a 0 return from these functions?
Can the updateReserve step oscillation be reliably triggered?
validation_requirements: Fuzz test with extreme ratios to find non-convergent inputs
Verify return value is 0 (not last iteration value) when loop exhausts
Test downstream protocol behavior when receiving 0

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify reserve/ratio combinations that cause Newton's method to oscillate or diverge
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call the function through a downstream protocol that uses the result for pricing
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Exploit the 0 return value for zero-cost operations or oracle manipulation

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Oracle feeds corrupted, pricing broken for dependent protocols

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced — needs numerical analysis to find non-convergent inputs

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor for calls returning 0 from these functions
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Zero-value reserve outputs in pool operations
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Unusual ratio inputs targeting edge cases

threats.title: _authorizeUpgrade Passes Wrong Parameters to Aquifer, Permanently Bricking Upgrade Path
threats.hypothesis_description: _authorizeUpgrade calls `IAquifer(aquifer).wellImplementation(_getImplementation())` passing the current implementation address, but the Aquifer's wellImplementation mapping expects a proxy (well) address. For an EIP-1167 minimal proxy, the ERC1967 implementation slot (_getImplementation()) is never initialized (returns address(0)). So `wellImplementation(address(0))` returns address(0), which fails the `require(activeProxy == ___self)` check. Similarly, `wellImplementation(newImplmentation)` passes an implementation address where a proxy is expected. Both checks are structurally incorrect, making it impossible to ever upgrade a WellUpgradeable proxy.
threats.investigation_rationale: The upgrade path is the core value proposition of WellUpgradeable. If upgrades are permanently bricked, the contract fails its fundamental purpose. Additionally, this bricking accidentally prevents exploitation of OPS-H002, creating a fragile security dependency.

proof_of_signal.pattern: wellImplementation(_getImplementation()) passes implementation address to mapping expecting proxy address; ERC1967 slot never initialized for minimal proxies

preconditions: WellUpgradeable proxy bored through Aquifer using EIP-1167 pattern
ERC1967 implementation slot not explicitly set during init (confirmed: __UUPSUpgradeable_init is empty)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Variable named `activeProxy` at line 58 stores result of wellImplementation(implementation_address) — naming reveals intent mismatch
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Affects all upgrade attempts regardless of caller

operational_questions: Does __UUPSUpgradeable_init() set the ERC1967 slot in any OZ version?
Is there any other code path that sets the ERC1967 implementation slot?
What is the Aquifer's behavior for wellImplementation(address(0))?
validation_requirements: Deploy WellUpgradeable via Aquifer, call _getImplementation() on proxy — confirm returns address(0)
Attempt upgradeTo with valid new implementation — confirm revert
Verify Aquifer.wellImplementation(address(0)) returns address(0)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: N/A — this is a denial-of-service from code defect, not an attacker exploit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attempt any upgrade — confirm permanent revert

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Wells cannot be upgraded to fix bugs or add features
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires complete redeployment of the Well system with corrected code

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Any failed upgradeTo transactions on WellUpgradeable proxies

threats.title: Unrestricted init() Reinitializer Allows Anyone to Seize Ownership After initNoWellToken Deployment
threats.hypothesis_description: WellUpgradeable has two initialization functions: initNoWellToken() with `initializer` (version 1) and init() with `reinitializer(2)`. If the deployment sequence calls initNoWellToken during bore (setting version to 1), the init() function remains callable by anyone, as reinitializer(2) only requires version < 2. Since init() calls __Ownable_init(), the caller becomes the owner. Neither function has any access control beyond the initializer guard.
threats.investigation_rationale: The two-phase initialization pattern creates a window where ownership can be seized by any external caller. Since init() sets the owner via __Ownable_init() (which sets msg.sender as owner), an attacker calling init() first becomes the permanent owner of the Well.
deployment_assumptions: Deployment uses initNoWellToken (version 1) during bore, leaving init (version 2) callable
threats.config_evidence: No WellUpgradeable deployment script found in provided scripts. LibWellUpgradeableConstructor.encodeWellDeploymentData (referenced in WellDeployer.sol line 47) is not provided — cannot confirm which init is called during bore.

proof_of_signal.pattern: init() has reinitializer(2) but no access control; initNoWellToken() has initializer (version 1); if initNoWellToken called first, init() is callable by anyone

preconditions: WellUpgradeable was bored with initNoWellToken (version 1)
init() has not yet been called (version < 2)
Attacker calls init() before legitimate owner

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Two init functions with sequential version numbers; no access control on either beyond initializer guard
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From deployment (with initNoWellToken) until init() is legitimately called

operational_questions: Does LibWellUpgradeableConstructor.encodeWellDeploymentData encode initNoWellToken or init?
Are there deployed WellUpgradeable proxies with _getInitializedVersion() == 1?
What can the owner do via OwnableUpgradeable that affects pool security?
validation_requirements: Deploy WellUpgradeable via Aquifer with initNoWellToken, verify _getInitializedVersion() == 1
Call init() from non-deployer address, verify owner() == attacker
Enumerate all onlyOwner functions inherited from Well and OwnableUpgradeable

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for new WellUpgradeable deployments via Aquifer events
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Check _getInitializedVersion() on proxy — if 1, init() is callable
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call init(name, symbol) to become owner and initialize ERC20 state
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Use owner privileges for transferOwnership, or any onlyOwner functions

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Attacker becomes permanent owner of the Well
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Depends on onlyOwner capabilities in Well; at minimum controls ownership transfer
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot be reversed if attacker transfers ownership to address with no access

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: init() called in same block or shortly after bore

threats.title: updateReserve Arithmetic Underflow Causes DoS for Specific Price Targets
threats.hypothesis_description: In Stable2.updateReserve, when targetPrice > currentPrice, the function computes `reserve - maxStepSize * (targetPrice - currentPrice) / (highPrice - lowPrice)`. If Newton's iteration overshoots causing currentPrice to drift past the LUT range boundaries, the ratio (targetPrice - currentPrice) / (highPrice - lowPrice) can exceed 1, making the step size larger than maxStepSize and potentially larger than the reserve itself. In Solidity 0.8.x, this causes an arithmetic underflow revert, creating a DoS condition for calcReserveAtRatioSwap and calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity at specific price targets.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Newton step is not bounded. If the price overshoots on any iteration, the correction step on the next iteration can underflow. This blocks legitimate reserve calculations and could prevent oracle updates or liquidations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Subtraction `reserve - pd.maxStepSize * (pd.targetPrice - pd.currentPrice) / (pd.lutData.highPrice - pd.lutData.lowPrice)` has no bounds check; step can exceed reserve when price ratio > 1

preconditions: Target price near LUT boundary where initial estimate is poor
First Newton iteration overshoots, pushing currentPrice outside [lowPrice, highPrice]
Resulting step size exceeds current reserve value

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No clamping or min() applied to step calculation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — triggered by specific input combinations

operational_questions: Which specific target prices cause the overshoot?
Does this affect oracle updates or other critical protocol paths?
Can an attacker manipulate pool reserves to push the target price into the DoS range?
validation_requirements: Fuzz test calcReserveAtRatioSwap with prices near LUT boundaries
Verify underflow revert occurs for specific inputs
Test with prices near the large LUT gaps (e.g., 0.001083e6 to 0.213318e6)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify target prices that cause Newton overshoot via off-chain simulation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Manipulate pool reserves to create a state where dependent protocols call calcReserveAtRatio with the DoS price
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Protocol operations that depend on this calculation revert
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Pool becomes non-functional for operations requiring ratio calculations
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires ability to influence pool reserves or wait for natural price movement

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Reserve-at-ratio calculations blocked for specific price ranges
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires rebalancing pool to move price out of DoS range
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Oracle updates, Beanstalk conversions, any ratio-based calculations

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium — requires positioning pool at specific state
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When pool price approaches LUT boundary gaps

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed oracle updates at extreme prices
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large swaps pushing price toward LUT boundaries

threats.title: calcLpTokenSupply Returns Imprecise Value on Non-Convergence Without Reverting
threats.hypothesis_description: calcLpTokenSupply iterates Newton's method up to 255 times to find D (LP token supply). If convergence is not achieved, the function returns the last computed lpTokenSupply value without reverting, unlike calcReserve which explicitly reverts. This imprecise D value propagates to calcReserveAtRatioSwap (which uses it for parityReserve), _calcRate, and all downstream pricing. With carefully crafted extremely imbalanced reserves, convergence can be slowed to return an imprecise D.
threats.investigation_rationale: calcReserve has an explicit revert for non-convergence (line 113), demonstrating the developers intended convergence failures to be fatal. The absence of this revert in calcLpTokenSupply is inconsistent and potentially exploitable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: For-loop at line 83 iterates 255 times; no revert after loop; lpTokenSupply retains last iteration value. Compare to calcReserve line 113: `revert('did not find convergence')`

preconditions: Extremely imbalanced reserves that slow Newton convergence
255 iterations insufficient for convergence to within ±1

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Inconsistent error handling: calcReserve reverts on non-convergence, calcLpTokenSupply does not

operational_questions: What reserve ratios cause calcLpTokenSupply to not converge in 255 iterations?
How imprecise is the returned value at iteration 255?
Can the imprecision be large enough to cause meaningful economic damage?
validation_requirements: Fuzz test with extreme reserve ratios to find non-convergent cases
Measure the error magnitude of the last iteration value vs true D
Trace the imprecision through calcReserveAtRatioSwap to quantify downstream impact

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Find reserve combinations where calcLpTokenSupply doesn't converge
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Calculate the imprecision of the returned D value
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Use this imprecise D in downstream ratio/reserve calculations
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Extract value from the resulting mispricing
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires ability to set or influence pool reserves

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Imprecise LP supply feeds into all pricing calculations

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unusual LP supply values relative to reserves
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large single-sided deposits creating extreme imbalance

threats.title: calcLpTokenSupply Division-by-Zero Revert When One Reserve Is Zero and Other Is Non-Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: calcLpTokenSupply checks `if (reserves[0] == 0 && reserves[1] == 0) return 0` but does not handle the case where only one reserve is zero. When one reserve is zero and the other is non-zero, the function proceeds to the Newton iteration where `dP = dP * lpTokenSupply / (scaledReserves[0] * N)`. If scaledReserves[0] is 0, this divides by zero and reverts. This causes unhandled reverts in any function that calls calcLpTokenSupply with one depleted reserve.
threats.investigation_rationale: A pool can naturally reach a state where one reserve is depleted (e.g., extreme one-sided withdrawal or swap). In this state, all operations depending on calcLpTokenSupply revert with an unhelpful error, potentially locking remaining funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line 78: check only handles both-zero case. Line 85: `dP * lpTokenSupply / (scaledReserves[0] * N)` divides by zero when scaledReserves[0] is 0

preconditions: One reserve is 0 and the other is non-zero
Any function calls calcLpTokenSupply with this state

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Only AND condition checked for zero reserves; single-zero case falls through to division

operational_questions: Can a pool naturally reach a single-zero-reserve state through normal operations?
Does the Well contract prevent complete draining of one reserve?
Are withdrawal/swap operations blocked when this state is approached?
validation_requirements: Call calcLpTokenSupply with [0, 1e18] — confirm revert
Verify whether Well.sol prevents single-reserve depletion
Test if this DoS blocks remaining LP withdrawals

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Drain one reserve to near-zero through repeated swaps
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Any subsequent call to calcLpTokenSupply reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Remaining LPs cannot withdraw because LP supply calculation fails
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Funds are temporarily locked until liquidity is added back
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Sufficient capital to drain one reserve

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Pool operations frozen when one reserve is depleted
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires adding liquidity to restore non-zero reserves
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All pool operations dependent on LP supply calculation

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When pool is small or unbalanced

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reserve approaching zero in pool state
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large one-sided swaps draining a reserve

threats.title: _calcRate Underflow at Extreme Reserve Ratios Where PRICE_PRECISION Perturbation Is Ineffective
threats.hypothesis_description: _calcRate computes rate as `reserves[i] - calcReserve(_reserves, i, lpTokenSupply, ...)` after incrementing reserves[j] by PRICE_PRECISION (1e6). For very large scaled reserves (18 decimals), adding 1e6 is negligible. If the perturbation is too small to change the reserve output, calcReserve returns a value >= reserves[i], causing the subtraction to underflow and revert. This would DoS calcRate and all functions depending on it (calcReserveAtRatioSwap, calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity).
threats.investigation_rationale: The rate calculation uses a numerical derivative with a fixed step size. When reserves are very large, the step becomes negligible relative to the reserve magnitude, breaking the derivative approximation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line 264: `rate = _reserves[i] - calcReserve(...)` — subtraction underflows if calcReserve returns >= _reserves[i]; PRICE_PRECISION (1e6) added at line 262 to 18-decimal scaled reserve

preconditions: Scaled reserves are large enough that adding 1e6 doesn't change calcReserve output
Or reserves are at an extreme ratio where the stableswap curve behavior inverts

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Fixed PRICE_PRECISION step size applied to variable-magnitude reserves
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: At extreme pool sizes or ratios

operational_questions: At what reserve magnitude does the 1e6 perturbation become ineffective?
Are there realistic pool sizes that hit this threshold?
Does the ±1 precision in calcReserve contribute to this issue?
validation_requirements: Test _calcRate with reserves of 1e30+ to check for underflow
Test with extreme reserve ratios (e.g., 1000:1)
Verify whether calcReserve's ±1 precision can cause underflow at normal scales

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify reserve magnitudes or ratios where _calcRate underflows
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Position pool at those reserves through large deposits or swaps
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Pool becomes non-functional for ratio calculations
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires significant capital to reach extreme pool states

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Rate calculations fail, blocking oracle updates and ratio-based operations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Rebalancing pool back to normal range

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Rate calculation reverts on large pools
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large deposits pushing reserves to extreme magnitudes

threats.title: LUT Price Range Limits Create DoS for Pools at Extreme Price Ratios
threats.hypothesis_description: Stable2LUT1 reverts for prices outside [0.001083e6, 10.37089e6] (liquidity) and [0.001083e6, 10.709509e6] (swap). If pool reserves create a target price outside these ranges, calcReserveAtRatioSwap and calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity revert with 'LUT: Invalid price'. This can block critical protocol operations (oracle updates, conversions, liquidations) when the pool is at extreme price ratios. The gap between the minimum valid price (1083) and the lowest data point is also very large, causing poor initial estimates and potential non-convergence in that range.
threats.investigation_rationale: A stableswap pool with A=100 can reach extreme price ratios if one side is heavily depleted. The static LUT range limits create hard boundaries beyond which the protocol cannot function.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `if (price < 0.001083e6) revert('LUT: Invalid price')` and at end of tree `revert('LUT: Invalid price')` for prices above maximum

preconditions: Pool reserves create a price ratio outside the LUT range (>10.7x or <0.001x)
Protocol operation requires calcReserveAtRatio for this price

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Hard revert boundaries in LUT with no graceful degradation
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When pool price exceeds LUT range

operational_questions: What reserve ratios correspond to the LUT boundary prices?
Can a pool naturally reach these extremes through normal trading?
What protocol operations are blocked by this DoS?
validation_requirements: Calculate reserves that produce target prices at/beyond LUT boundaries
Verify revert behavior at boundary prices
Test impact on Beanstalk oracle and conversion operations

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Push pool reserves to extreme ratio through large one-sided swaps
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Target price falls outside LUT range
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: calcReserveAtRatio functions revert, blocking dependent operations
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Pool oracle freezes, blocking conversions or liquidations
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Significant capital to push price outside range

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Pool oracle and ratio calculations fail at extreme prices
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires rebalancing pool to bring price within LUT range
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Oracle updates, Beanstalk conversions, any ratio-dependent operation

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium — requires substantial capital for price manipulation
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: During low liquidity or high volatility
